name: Bottleneck
definition: Resource with minimum capacity; constrains system performance
explanation15: Picture 5 friends passing a basketball down a line — but one kid is way slower than the rest. No matter how fast everyone else is, the whole chain moves at the slowest kid's speed. That's a bottleneck. The cruel part? Improving anyone EXCEPT the bottleneck is completely wasted effort — you'll just pile up more balls waiting in front of the slow kid. On the exam, your first job is always: find the bottleneck. Everything else follows from there.
formulas: Flow rate = min(demand rate, process capacity)
appearsIn: Kristen's Cookie Company After Class Handouts
keyInsights: The bottleneck determines the capacity of the entire system
Bottlenecks can shift when order sizes change
May have multiple bottlenecks in complex systems

name: Process Capacity
definition: = Bottleneck capacity; max output rate of entire system
explanation15: Process capacity is like asking 'how fast can this factory POSSIBLY go?' — and the answer is always the speed of its weakest link. If your sandwich shop has a bread-slicer doing 120/hour, a filler doing 100/hour, and a wrapper doing 80/hour, your process capacity is 80/hour. Period. It doesn't matter that the slicer is blazing fast — the wrapper sets the speed limit for the entire system. This is the first number you calculate in any capacity problem.
appearsIn: Kristen's Cookie Company After Class Handouts
keyInsights: Process capacity equals the capacity of the bottleneck resource

name: Flow Rate
definition: = min(Demand Rate, Process Capacity)
explanation15: Think of flow rate as the 'actual speed' vs. the 'speed limit.' Your factory might be capable of 100 units/hour (process capacity), but if customers only want 60, you'll only make 60. Flip it: if customers want 150 but you can only make 100, you're capped at 100. Flow rate = min(demand, capacity) — it's whichever constraint bites first. The key exam move is figuring out whether you're demand-constrained or capacity-constrained, because the improvement strategy is completely different for each.
formulas: Flow rate = min(demand rate, process capacity)
appearsIn: Kristen's Cookie Company After Class Handouts
keyInsights: Flow rate is constrained by either demand or capacity
When demand exceeds capacity, you're capacity-constrained
When capacity exceeds demand, you're demand-constrained

name: Utilization
definition: = Flow Rate / Capacity = Time Busy / Time Available
explanation15: Utilization is like checking how much of your phone battery you're using. At 50% utilization, a server has plenty of breathing room — like a highway at 2 AM. At 95%, it's rush hour and one fender-bender causes a 3-mile backup. Here's the counter-intuitive part: high utilization FEELS efficient but actually creates massive waiting times when anything varies even slightly. Emergency rooms and fire stations intentionally run at LOW utilization — because when you need them, you need them NOW, not 'after the current 19 patients.' On exams, utilization connects process analysis (flow rate/capacity) to queueing (where it drives the hockey-stick wait curve).
appearsIn: Kristen's Cookie Company After Class Handouts
keyInsights: 100% utilization feels efficient but creates vulnerability
High utilization + variability = dramatically increased waiting times
Emergency services intentionally run low utilization for responsiveness

name: Throughput Time (Flow Time)
definition: Time for a job to enter and exit the system
explanation15: Imagine timing yourself from the moment you join the Chipotle line to when you walk out with your burrito. That's throughput time — the customer's TOTAL experience, including all the boring waiting AND the actual work. The sneaky thing is that most of throughput time is usually waiting, not processing. A hospital visit might involve 8 minutes of actual doctor time inside 3 hours of total time. Throughput time is what customers feel, which is why it matters so much — and it's one of the three variables in Little's Law, so if you know it plus throughput rate, you instantly get WIP.
formulas: WIP = Throughput Rate × Throughput Time (Little's Law)
keyInsights: Also called Flow Time or Turn-Around Time
Includes both processing and waiting time
Key component of Little's Law

name: Cycle Time
definition: Time between successive completions; = 1/Capacity
explanation15: Cycle time is the heartbeat of production — the rhythm between finished units rolling off the line. If a pizza comes out of the oven every 12 minutes, that's your cycle time. It's simply 1/capacity: a machine that makes 10 units/hour has a 6-minute cycle time. Don't confuse it with throughput time (the total journey time for one unit) — cycle time is about the GAP between outputs, not how long one unit takes. The system's cycle time equals the bottleneck's cycle time, which is why bottleneck identification is step one in every problem.
appearsIn: Kristen's Cookie Company After Class Handouts
keyInsights: Cycle time is the reciprocal of capacity
The system cycle time equals the cycle time of the bottleneck

name: Lead Time
definition: Time from order placement to fulfillment
explanation15: Lead time is the customer's clock — it starts ticking when they hit 'order' and stops when the package lands on their doorstep. It includes everything: order processing, queue time, manufacturing, shipping, and any delays in between. It's broader than throughput time because it can include time before the item even enters your production system (like waiting for raw materials). For businesses competing on delivery speed (Amazon Prime, Domino's 30-minute guarantee), lead time is THE competitive weapon.
appearsIn: Kristen's Cookie Company After Class Handouts
keyInsights: Includes order processing, production, and delivery time
Different from throughput time - measured from customer perspective

name: Setup Time
definition: Time to prepare for new task; one of the '3 pain points'
explanation15: Setup time is the 'switching cost' of production — like the 20 minutes a barista spends recalibrating the espresso machine when switching from lattes to pour-overs. During setup, the machine produces NOTHING, which eats into your effective capacity. This creates a painful dilemma: long setups push you toward big batches (to amortize the dead time), but big batches mean customers wait longer for variety. Reducing setup time — through better tooling, practice, or parallel preparation (like F1 pit crews) — is one of the highest-leverage improvements in operations. Setup time is also the hidden 'S' in EOQ's ordering cost, connecting process analysis to inventory theory.
formulas: Setup time is included in cycle time calculations
keyInsights: One of the three main pain points in operations
Reduces effective capacity
Fixed ordering cost in EOQ is essentially a setup cost
Can be reduced through training, standardization, parallelization

name: Capacity Analysis
definition: Tool: Determine capacity of each resource in isolation, identify bottleneck
explanation15: Capacity analysis is your detective toolkit — you time each step independently, calculate its capacity (units/time), and find who's the slowest. It's like timing each runner on a relay team to figure out who's holding back the whole squad. The method is simple: capacity of a step = number of workers (or machines) ÷ processing time per unit. But watch for traps: setup times that change with batch size, shared resources that serve multiple steps, and workers who do different tasks. This is the foundation of literally every process problem on the exam — if you can't do capacity analysis cleanly, nothing else works.
formulas: Capacity of station = Number of workers / Processing time per unit
appearsIn: Kristen's Cookie Company After Class Handouts
keyInsights: Foundation for all process analysis
Must consider setup times and batch effects
Bottleneck identification drives improvement priorities

name: Product-Process Matrix
definition: Volume/variety vs. process flexibility tradeoff
explanation15: Think about the spectrum from a custom tailor (one-of-a-kind suits, slow, expensive) to a T-shirt factory (millions of identical shirts, fast, cheap). The Product-Process Matrix maps this tradeoff: high variety needs flexible job-shop processes, while high volume needs rigid assembly lines. The dangerous zone is the middle — trying to mass-produce custom products or hand-craft commodity items. Companies that stray off the diagonal (like Beleza Natural trying salon-quality hair care at massive scale) face a strategic puzzle: do you standardize the process or limit the product range?
keyInsights: Volume and variety are generally incompatible
Different positions require different operational capabilities
Misalignment between product and process leads to inefficiency

name: Operations Strategy (CQDF)
definition: Cost, Quality, Delivery, Flexibility — define, rank, measure
explanation15: CQDF is like choosing your character class in a video game — you get to prioritize Cost, Quality, Delivery, or Flexibility, but you can't max out all four. Walmart chose Cost. Apple chose Quality. Amazon Prime chose Delivery. A local print shop chose Flexibility. The real power is that once you RANK these priorities, every operational decision has a clear tiebreaker. Should we add overtime? Depends — are we optimizing for cost or delivery speed? Southwest Airlines ranked Quality #1, Delivery #2, Cost #3, Flexibility #4, and every management lever (one plane type, no seat assignments, point-to-point routes) directly supports that ranking. On the exam, always connect operational tactics back to CQDF priorities.
keyInsights: Must define, rank, and measure each competitive priority
Cannot excel at all four simultaneously
Operations must align with strategic priorities
Southwest: Quality #1, Delivery #2, Cost #3, Flexibility #4

name: Management Levers
definition: Operational tactics that support strategic objectives
explanation15: Management levers are the knobs and dials you turn to execute your strategy — things like facility location, technology investment, workforce policies, quality systems, and capacity decisions. Think of CQDF as choosing your destination on a map, and management levers as the steering wheel, gas pedal, and route choices that get you there. The key insight is ALIGNMENT: every lever should pull in the direction of your strategic priorities. Southwest's single aircraft type (lever) reduces maintenance costs and increases schedule flexibility (supports their CQDF ranking). When levers contradict your strategy — like a low-cost airline buying luxury lounges — something's broken.
keyInsights: Must align with strategic objectives
Include facility, capacity, technology, workforce, and quality decisions

name: Bottleneck Shifting
definition: Doubling bottleneck capacity may not double system capacity
explanation15: Here's the trap that catches everyone: you find the bottleneck, double its capacity, and expect output to double. But it doesn't — because now a DIFFERENT step becomes the new bottleneck! It's like fixing the narrow bridge on a highway only to discover the on-ramp is now the chokepoint. Bottleneck shifting means improvements have diminishing returns, and you often need to improve multiple steps to get the full benefit. On exam problems, after you 'fix' the bottleneck, ALWAYS re-check which step is now the constraint. The system's behavior can change dramatically with each improvement.
keyInsights: Bottlenecks can shift when capacity changes
System improvement requires considering all constraints
May have multiple bottlenecks operating simultaneously

name: Focused Operations
definition: Rigid, well-defined processes for narrow product offering at low cost
explanation15: Focused operations is the 'do one thing and be world-class at it' philosophy. Narayana Heart Hospital ONLY does heart surgeries — and because of that extreme focus, they've driven costs to 1/10th of US hospitals while maintaining equal or better outcomes. It's like how In-N-Out has a tiny menu but executes flawlessly, while a diner with 200 items struggles with quality on all of them. Focus enables specialization, learning curves, and ruthless process optimization. The tradeoff? You need enough volume in that one thing to justify the specialization, and you sacrifice flexibility entirely.
keyInsights: Achieves cost advantages through specialization
Requires high volume to justify specialization
Example: Narayana Heart Hospital vs. General Hospital

name: Little's Law
definition: WIP = Throughput Rate × Throughput Time
explanation15: Imagine a nightclub with a line outside. If 100 people are inside (WIP) and each stays for 2 hours (flow time), then 50 people per hour must be entering and leaving (throughput). That's Little's Law: WIP = Throughput × Flow Time. It's shockingly universal — it works for factories, hospitals, Starbucks lines, even email inboxes. The magic is that if you know any two of the three variables, you get the third for free. No assumptions about distributions, arrival patterns, or service rules needed — it just works for any stable system. This is the Swiss Army knife of operations: when you're stuck on an exam problem, ask yourself 'Can Little's Law unlock the missing variable?'
keyInsights: Universal law that applies to any stable system
If you know any two variables, you can calculate the third
Foundation for understanding Factory Physics relationships

name: WIP (Work in Process)
definition: Number of jobs in the system at any time
explanation15: WIP is like counting how many cars are currently on a stretch of highway — not the ones that already exited, not the ones that haven't entered yet, just the ones IN the system right now. In a restaurant, it's every order between 'placed' and 'served.' Here's the counterintuitive part: adding more WIP doesn't always help. Up to a point, more WIP keeps machines busy and increases throughput. But past the bottleneck's limit, extra WIP just piles up like cars in a traffic jam — throughput flatlines while wait times skyrocket. Finding the 'sweet spot' WIP level (enough to keep the bottleneck fed, not so much that everything clogs) is the central puzzle of Factory Physics.
keyInsights: Inventory in process, not finished goods
Higher WIP generally increases throughput time
Must balance WIP levels - too little hurts throughput, too much hurts time

name: Variability Effect
definition: Variability decreases throughput rate
explanation15: Picture two checkout lines at a grocery store. Line A: customers arrive exactly every 3 minutes and each takes exactly 2 minutes to check out — smooth, no waiting. Line B: customers arrive randomly (sometimes 3 in one minute, then nobody for 5 minutes) and some have 2 items while others have 200 — chaos, long waits. Same average arrival rate, same average service time, but Line B performs WAY worse. That's the variability effect: randomness ALWAYS degrades performance below the theoretical best. The more variable your system, the bigger the gap between what you COULD do and what you actually achieve. This is why Toyota obsesses over standardization — reducing variability is free capacity.
formulas: Variability is captured in queueing formulas via coefficient of variation
keyInsights: Variability always degrades system performance
Effect is captured in Factory Physics curves
Leads to need for buffers or reduced throughput

name: WIP-Throughput Tradeoff
definition: More WIP ↑ throughput rate (to bottleneck max) but ↑ throughput time
explanation15: This is the fundamental 'pick your poison' of operations. Imagine a coffee shop: if you let orders pile up (high WIP), your baristas are never idle and throughput is maximized — but customers wait forever. If you limit orders (low WIP), customers get served fast — but baristas sometimes stand around with nothing to do, and throughput drops. You literally cannot optimize both simultaneously when variability exists. The Factory Physics curves show this graphically: as WIP increases, throughput rises toward the bottleneck max but then flattens, while throughput time rises and never stops climbing. Your job is to find the WIP level where the tradeoff best matches your business priorities.
keyInsights: Fundamental tradeoff in all systems with variability
Shown graphically in Factory Physics curves
Must choose optimal WIP level based on priorities

name: 'Buffer or Suffer'
definition: Must hold WIP buffer or accept reduced throughput
explanation15: Buffer or Suffer is Factory Physics' most brutally honest principle: in a world with variability, you MUST hold some kind of buffer — inventory, extra capacity, or extra time — or you WILL lose throughput. There's no magical third option. It's like keeping an umbrella in your car: it takes up space and you rarely use it, but when it rains, you're glad it's there. The Grohe Lux case nailed this: when they slashed WIP to look 'lean,' throughput dropped because machines starved for work. They removed the buffer and suffered. On the exam, if someone asks 'why not just eliminate all inventory?' — this is your answer.
keyInsights: Core principle of Factory Physics
Grohe Lux example: reducing WIP reduced throughput time but also throughput rate
Cannot eliminate all waste without sacrificing performance

name: Queueing Model (Single Server)
explanation15: The queueing formula predicts how long people wait in line. It shows that as a server gets busier (higher utilization), waiting times grow dramatically. It also shows that unpredictable arrivals (like random customer arrivals) or unpredictable service times make waits much longer. It's why McDonald's trains for consistent service times.
keyInsights: Waiting time increases dramatically as utilization approaches 100%
Variability in arrivals and service magnifies waiting
Foundation for service system design

name: Queueing Model (Multi Server)
explanation15: The multi-server queueing formula shows how much faster service gets when you add more servers (like opening more checkout lanes). Adding a second server doesn't just cut wait time in half - it often cuts it much more because you also reduce the randomness effects. That's why grocery stores open more lanes during rush hour.
keyInsights: Adding servers provides more than proportional improvement
Pooling effect reduces overall variability impact
Must consider cost of additional servers vs. waiting cost reduction

name: Utilization Effect
definition: ρ/(1-ρ) — waiting time grows dramatically as utilization → 1
explanation15: The utilization effect is like a hockey stick - as utilization goes from 80% to 90% to 95%, waiting times don't just increase a little, they explode exponentially. At 95% utilization, you wait 19 times longer than at 50% utilization! This is why emergency rooms and fire departments intentionally run at low utilization.
keyInsights: Exponential growth in waiting as utilization approaches 100%
Hockey stick shape of utilization vs. wait time curve
Why 100% utilization is dangerous in service systems

name: Variance Effect
definition: (Ca² + Cs²)/2 — variability in arrivals/service magnifies utilization
explanation15: The variance effect shows that unpredictability makes everything worse. If customers arrive randomly (high Ca) or service times vary a lot (high Cs), waiting times increase. Appointments reduce Ca (arrival variability), and training reduces Cs (service variability). A system with no variability (Ca = Cs = 0) has no waiting at all!
formulas: Ca = σa/A (coefficient of variation for arrivals)
Cs = σs/M (coefficient of variation for service)
keyInsights: Variability always makes waiting worse
Both arrival and service variability matter
Appointments and training are key improvement strategies

name: Coefficient of Variation
definition: Ca = σa/A; Cs = σs/M — standardized variability measure
explanation15: Coefficient of variation is like measuring 'how random' something is, adjusted for its average. If bus arrivals average 10 minutes with 5-minute variation, that's much more random (CV = 0.5) than if they average 60 minutes with 5-minute variation (CV = 0.083). It lets you compare variability across different situations.
keyInsights: Standardized measure of variability
CV = 1 indicates high variability
CV = 0 indicates no variability (deterministic)

name: Psychology of Waiting
definition: 8 principles for managing customer perception of wait times
explanation15: Psychology of waiting recognizes that how long people think they waited matters more than actual time. Occupied time feels shorter (magazines in waiting rooms), uncertain waits feel longer (show estimated times), and unfair waits feel terrible (single line feeding multiple servers). Disney is the master of this - making 45-minute waits feel acceptable through entertainment and clear expectations.
keyInsights: Occupied time feels shorter than unoccupied time
Uncertain waits feel longer than known waits
Unfair waits feel worse than fair waits
Solo waits feel longer than group waits

name: Operations Triangle
definition: Tradeoff between Cost, Delivery, Flexibility — and 'bad stuff' (queues, WIP, wait)
explanation15: The Operations Triangle shows you can't have everything. You can be fast and flexible but not cheap, or cheap and fast but not flexible. The 'bad stuff' - waiting, inventory, complexity - sits in the middle as the price you pay. Southwest Airlines chose fast and cheap, accepting limited flexibility (only one plane type, no seat assignments).
keyInsights: Cannot optimize all three dimensions simultaneously
Must choose two priorities and accept constraints on the third
Bad stuff (queues, inventory, waste) is the price of the tradeoff

name: Bullwhip Effect
definition: Order variability amplifies moving up supply chain from consumer
explanation15: The bullwhip effect is like the telephone game but with orders. A small change in customer demand (say, 10% increase) causes retailers to order 15% more from distributors, who then order 25% more from manufacturers, who order 40% more from suppliers. By the time it reaches the top of the supply chain, a tiny demand change has become a huge swing in orders.
appearsIn: eBeer Game Debrief After Class Handouts
keyInsights: Small demand changes amplify upstream
Creates boom-bust cycles in manufacturing
Observed in beer distribution studies at MIT

name: Causes of Bullwhip
definition: Over-reactive ordering, trade promotions, shortage gaming, delay times, no communication
explanation15: Bullwhip happens because everyone in the supply chain acts on incomplete information and makes reasonable but amplifying decisions. Retailers see demand spike and order extra 'just in case.' Distributors see big orders and think demand is really growing, so they order even more. Trade promotions make everyone stock up when prices are low, creating artificial demand spikes.
appearsIn: eBeer Game Debrief After Class Handouts
keyInsights: Over-reactive ordering based on incomplete information
Trade promotions create artificial demand spikes
Shortage gaming - ordering extra when expecting allocation
Information delays compound the problem

name: Counter-Strategies (Bullwhip)
definition: S&OP, CPFR, VMI, EDLP, limit order sizes, penalize cancellations
explanation15: Counter-strategies attack each cause of bullwhip directly. Share information so everyone sees real consumer demand (CPFR). Let suppliers manage inventory so they control ordering (VMI). Use everyday low pricing instead of promotions (EDLP). Set limits on how much orders can increase period-to-period. Make cancellations expensive so people don't over-order.
appearsIn: eBeer Game Debrief After Class Handouts
keyInsights: Information sharing reduces guessing
Vendor managed inventory eliminates ordering games
Everyday low pricing reduces forward buying
Order size limits prevent over-reaction

name: S&OP (Sales & Operations Planning)
definition: Internal information sharing — weekly cross-functional meetings
explanation15: S&OP is like having a family meeting every week where parents (sales and operations) share what they know and plan together. Sales says 'I think we'll sell 1000 units next month' and operations says 'I can make 800 with current capacity, but 1200 if we add overtime.' They make decisions together instead of separately.
appearsIn: eBeer Game Debrief After Class Handouts
keyInsights: Internal coordination between sales and operations
Regular cross-functional meetings
Aligns capacity planning with demand forecasts

name: CPFR
definition: Coordinated Planning, Forecasting and Replenishment — external sharing
explanation15: CPFR is like S&OP but between companies. Walmart and Procter & Gamble sit down together and share forecasts: 'We think Tide sales will increase 15% in Q4 due to our promotion,' and P&G responds 'OK, we'll increase production and have inventory ready.' Instead of guessing what the other will do, they plan together.
appearsIn: eBeer Game Debrief After Class Handouts
keyInsights: External coordination between supply chain partners
Shared forecasting and planning
Reduces information delays and guessing

name: VMI (Vendor Managed Inventory)
definition: Supplier manages customer's inventory using shared data
explanation15: VMI is like having your grocery supplier stock your pantry directly. Instead of you deciding when to order more cereal, the cereal company monitors your pantry, knows your family's eating patterns, and automatically delivers more when you're getting low. They take over the ordering decisions because they have better information and expertise.
appearsIn: eBeer Game Debrief After Class Handouts
keyInsights: Supplier takes responsibility for customer's inventory levels
Requires shared point-of-sale data
Eliminates customer ordering decisions and bullwhip

name: Supply Chain Risk Management
definition: Anticipatory strategies (buy options) → Reactive adaptations (exercise options)
explanation15: Supply chain risk management is like buying insurance before you need it. Anticipatory strategies are preparations you make when things are calm - like having backup suppliers or extra inventory. Reactive strategies are what you do when disaster strikes - like switching to the backup supplier or changing your product. You 'buy options' in good times and 'exercise' them in bad times.
appearsIn: Supply Chain Risk Management After Class Handouts
keyInsights: Two-stage approach: anticipatory then reactive
Anticipatory strategies create options for later use
Must balance cost of preparation vs. risk reduction

name: Risk Mitigation Strategies
definition: Footprint restructuring, multi-region sourcing, China+1, strategic partnerships, vertical integration
explanation15: Risk mitigation is like not putting all your eggs in one basket, but for supply chains. Instead of buying everything from China, you also source from Vietnam (China+1). Instead of one giant factory, you have several smaller ones in different regions. If there's a problem in one place - earthquake, trade war, pandemic - you can still operate from somewhere else.
appearsIn: Supply Chain Risk Management After Class Handouts
keyInsights: Geographic diversification reduces regional risks
China+1 strategies reduce single-country dependence
Vertical integration provides more control but less flexibility

name: Safety Stock / Buffers
definition: Inventory/time buffers against demand variability
explanation15: Safety stock is like keeping extra food in your pantry in case unexpected guests arrive or the grocery store runs out of something you need. In supply chains, it's extra inventory you hold 'just in case' demand is higher than expected or deliveries are late. It costs money to hold, but prevents stockouts.
appearsIn: Supply Chain Risk Management After Class Handouts
keyInsights: Buffer against demand and supply uncertainty
Costs money to hold but prevents stockouts
Alternative to safety stock is safety time (longer lead times)

name: EOQ (Economic Order Quantity)
definition: EOQ = √(2SD/H); minimizes total relevant cost of repeat ordering
explanation15: EOQ is like figuring out the perfect size shopping cart at Costco. If you shop too often with a small cart, you waste time and gas on many trips (high setup costs). If you shop rarely with a giant cart, food spoils and your garage fills up (high holding costs). EOQ finds the sweet spot that minimizes your total annual costs.
keyInsights: Balances ordering costs vs. holding costs
EOQ is robust - being off by 25% only costs ~3% more
At EOQ, ordering cost equals holding cost

name: Total Relevant Cost
definition: TC = S(D/Q) + H(Q/2); ordering cost + holding cost
explanation15: Total relevant cost is like calculating your total annual cost of shopping. It includes the 'hassle cost' of making trips to the store (more trips if you buy small amounts each time) plus the 'storage cost' of keeping stuff at home (more storage cost if you buy large amounts and hold them longer). You want to minimize the sum of both.
keyInsights: Sum of ordering and holding costs
U-shaped curve with minimum at EOQ
At EOQ, ordering cost = holding cost

name: Holding Cost (H)
definition: H = ic + h; financial (cost of capital × unit cost) + physical holding
explanation15: Holding cost is like the 'rent' you pay for keeping inventory. Financial holding cost is the money you could have earned if you invested the cash instead of buying inventory (opportunity cost). Physical holding cost is warehouse rent, insurance, spoilage, theft, etc. If something costs $100 and your company's cost of capital is 20%, that's $20/year just in financial costs.
formulas: h = physical holding cost per unit
keyInsights: Combines financial and physical holding costs
Financial cost = opportunity cost of capital
Physical cost = warehousing, insurance, obsolescence, theft

name: EOQ Robustness / Savings
definition: % savings = [TC(Q) - TC(EOQ)] / TC(Q); EOQ is robust to errors
explanation15: EOQ robustness means that being slightly wrong doesn't hurt much. If the optimal order quantity is 100 but you order 125, your total costs only increase by about 3%. This is because the total cost curve is very flat near the optimum - like a wide, shallow bowl. So don't stress about getting EOQ exactly right.
keyInsights: EOQ is very forgiving of errors
Cost curve is flat near the optimum
Being 25% off EOQ typically costs less than 3% extra

name: Consolidation Effect (EOQ)
definition: Merging demand → EOQ scales by √n, so TC grows sub-linearly → savings
explanation15: EOQ consolidation is like combining your grocery shopping with your neighbor's. If you each need 100 items per month, together you need 200 items. But the optimal shopping frequency doesn't double - it only increases by √2 = 1.4x. So instead of each making 4 trips per month, together you might make 6 trips total, saving 2 trips. Bigger scale = efficiency gains.
formulas: EOQ scales by √n for n locations
Savings increase with number of locations consolidated
keyInsights: Consolidating demand creates square-root savings
Larger scale improves efficiency
Common question on exams - compare separate vs. consolidated operations

name: Newsvendor Model
definition: One-shot ordering under uncertain demand; Q* = NORM.INV(pc, μ, σ)
explanation15: The newsvendor model is like deciding how many newspapers to buy each morning when you don't know how many customers will want them. Buy too few and you miss sales (underage cost). Buy too many and you're stuck with unsold papers (overage cost). The model finds the order quantity that maximizes your expected profit given this uncertainty.
keyInsights: Applies to any one-time decision under uncertainty
Balances overage cost vs. underage cost
Uses normal distribution to handle demand uncertainty

name: Critical Fractile
definition: pc = Cu/(Cu + Co); probability demand ≤ optimal order quantity
explanation15: The critical fractile is like finding the 'break-even point' for stocking decisions. If you make $5 profit per item sold but lose $2 on each leftover, your critical fractile is 5/(5+2) = 71.4%. This means you should stock enough to satisfy 71.4% of possible demand scenarios. Stock more than that and overage costs outweigh benefits.
keyInsights: Ratio of underage cost to total costs
Determines optimal service level
Higher profit margin → higher critical fractile → stock more

name: Overage Cost (Co)
definition: Cost of ordering too much; typically = Cost - Salvage Value
explanation15: Overage cost is what you lose on each item you can't sell. If you buy newspapers for $1 each and unsold papers have no value, your overage cost is $1 per leftover paper. If you can return them for 30 cents, your overage cost is 70 cents. It's the financial pain of having too much inventory.
keyInsights: Financial loss per excess unit
Affected by salvage value possibilities
Lower overage cost → stock more aggressively

name: Underage Cost (Cu)
definition: Cost of ordering too little; typically = Price - Cost
explanation15: Underage cost is the opportunity cost of a lost sale - what you would have earned if you had the item in stock. If you sell newspapers for $3 that cost you $1, your underage cost is $2 per paper you could have sold but didn't have. It's the profit you lose when you run out.
keyInsights: Opportunity cost of lost sales
Equals profit margin per unit
Higher underage cost → stock more aggressively

name: Service Level
definition: = In-stock probability = P(demand ≤ Q); at Q*, equals critical fractile
explanation15: Service level is like your 'success rate' at having what customers want. 90% service level means that 90% of the time you have enough inventory, but 10% of the time you run out. At the optimal order quantity, your service level equals your critical fractile - the break-even point between stocking more vs. less.
formulas: Service level = NORM.DIST(Q, μ, σ, TRUE)
keyInsights: Probability of not stocking out
At optimal Q, service level = critical fractile
Higher service levels require exponentially more inventory

name: Normal Distribution / z-scores
definition: z = (Q - μ)/σ; NORM.INV, NORM.DIST, NORM.S.INV usage
explanation15: Normal distribution and z-scores are like standardizing test scores so you can compare across different tests. Instead of saying 'demand is usually 100 with variation of 20', you convert to z-scores to say 'this order quantity is 1.5 standard deviations above average demand.' This lets you use standard normal tables and Excel functions.
keyInsights: Standardizes different normal distributions
Enables use of standard normal table
Excel functions handle the conversions automatically

name: Postponement / Delayed Customization
definition: 'Vanilla box' — hold generic inventory, customize after demand known. σ_total = σ × √n
explanation15: Postponement is like a car dealer keeping unpainted cars and painting them based on customer orders, instead of guessing which colors to stock. By keeping generic 'vanilla' products and customizing late, you reduce total uncertainty. Instead of guessing demand for 5 different colors, you only predict total car demand - much easier and more accurate.
formulas: Combined uncertainty is less than sum of individual uncertainties
appearsIn: Supply Chain Strategies After Class Handouts
keyInsights: Delay customization until demand is known
Reduces inventory by pooling uncertainty
Enables mass customization at lower cost

name: Service Level Curve Shape
definition: As service level → 100%, order quantity increases exponentially (convex curve)
explanation15: The service level curve shows why 100% service is unrealistic and expensive. Going from 90% to 95% service might require 20% more inventory, but going from 95% to 99% might require 100% more inventory! The curve gets steeper and steeper, like climbing a cliff. This is why even Amazon doesn't promise 100% availability.
keyInsights: Exponential increase in inventory needed for high service levels
Explains why 100% service is rarely economical
Convex shape - gets steeper at high service levels

name: BrightSide Application
definition: Newsvendor with zero salvage; allocation of limited inventory; equitable service levels
explanation15: BrightSide is newsvendor with a twist - fresh produce that's worthless if unsold (zero salvage value). With multiple stores and limited supply, you must allocate inventory fairly. Instead of giving one store 99% service and another 50%, you aim for equal service levels across stores, which means bigger stores get proportionally more inventory.
keyInsights: Zero salvage value changes the economics
Allocation problem with fairness constraints
Equal service levels, not equal absolute quantities

name: Revenue Management
definition: Newsvendor applied to airlines: controlling low-fare vs. high-fare seats
explanation15: Revenue management is like a restaurant deciding how many tables to save for walk-in customers vs. reservations. Airlines use newsvendor logic to decide how many cheap seats to sell early vs. expensive seats to save for last-minute business travelers. Sell too many cheap seats and you turn away profitable customers. Save too many seats and the plane flies with empty seats.
keyInsights: Newsvendor applied to capacity allocation
Balances low-margin early sales vs. high-margin late sales
Used by airlines, hotels, restaurants

name: Fisher Matrix
definition: Functional products → Efficient SC; Innovative products → Responsive SC
explanation15: The Fisher Matrix is like matching your supply chain strategy to your product type. Functional products (toilet paper, pasta) have predictable demand, so you want an efficient supply chain focused on low cost. Innovative products (fashion, new electronics) have unpredictable demand, so you need a responsive supply chain that can react quickly to what sells.
appearsIn: Supply Chain Strategies After Class Handouts
keyInsights: Match supply chain type to product characteristics
Functional products need efficiency
Innovative products need responsiveness
Mismatch leads to either high costs or poor service

name: Efficient vs. Responsive SC
definition: Efficient = low cost, lean; Responsive = flexible, fast, buffers
explanation15: Efficient supply chains are like Walmart - focused on lowest cost through standardization, large scale, and minimal buffers. Responsive supply chains are like Amazon - focused on speed and flexibility with extra inventory, multiple suppliers, and fast shipping. You can't be both - responsive costs more, efficient is slower to adapt.
appearsIn: Supply Chain Strategies After Class Handouts
keyInsights: Efficient: minimize cost, lean operations, standardization
Responsive: maximize flexibility, speed, customer service
Trade-off between cost and responsiveness

name: Dual Response Strategy
definition: Stable factory (low cost) + Variable factory (flexible)
explanation15: Dual response is like having two kitchens - one industrial kitchen that efficiently makes your standard menu items in bulk, and one flexible kitchen that can quickly whip up custom orders and new dishes. You get both cost efficiency for predictable demand and responsiveness for uncertain demand, but you pay for two different capabilities.
appearsIn: Supply Chain Strategies After Class Handouts
keyInsights: Combine efficient and responsive strategies
Stable capacity for predictable demand
Flexible capacity for uncertain demand

name: Sport Obermeyer Strategies
definition: Forecast distributions, early order info, newsvendor for initial production, reactive capacity
explanation15: Sport Obermeyer shows newsvendor in action for fashion. They survey retailers early to forecast demand, use newsvendor model to decide initial production quantities, then react to early sales data to adjust production up or down. It's like a restaurant that pre-orders ingredients based on reservations, then adjusts portions based on how hungry the first customers seem.
appearsIn: Supply Chain Strategies After Class Handouts
keyInsights: Early information gathering to improve forecasts
Newsvendor for initial production decisions
Reactive capacity to adjust based on early sales signals

name: Setups
definition: Recurring theme: setup times reduce effective capacity and create batching incentives
explanation15: Setups are like the time you spend cleaning your kitchen between cooking different meals. If setups take long, you want to make big batches to 'amortize' that cleaning time. But big batches mean long wait times for variety. Setups appear everywhere - machine changeovers in manufacturing, ordering costs in EOQ, even context-switching time when people change tasks.
keyInsights: One of the three main pain points in operations
Creates batching incentives
Fixed ordering cost in EOQ is a setup cost
Can be reduced through SMED, training, and standardization

name: Bottlenecks
definition: Recurring theme: constraint resources that limit system performance
explanation15: Bottlenecks are like the narrowest part of an hourglass - they control how fast everything flows through the system. They appear in process capacity analysis, Factory Physics WIP curves, and Littlefield simulation. The key insight is that improving a bottleneck improves the whole system, but improving a non-bottleneck usually doesn't help at all.
keyInsights: One of the three main pain points in operations
Determines system capacity
Can shift when improvements are made
Focus improvement efforts on bottlenecks, not non-constraints

name: Uncertainty & Variability
definition: Recurring theme: variability degrades performance and requires buffers or flexibility
explanation15: Uncertainty and variability are like bad weather - they make everything harder and slower. Variable customer arrivals create queues. Variable demand creates inventory challenges. Variable processing times reduce throughput. The theme throughout operations is that variability hurts performance, so you either need buffers (inventory, capacity, time) or flexible systems that can adapt.
keyInsights: One of the three main pain points in operations
Always degrades system performance
Requires buffers (inventory, capacity, time) or flexibility
Source of many supply chain problems
